title: Legalism vs. Grace
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Legalism and Grace
summary: Scripture teaches that salvation comes through grace alone, not rule-keeping, while also insisting that genuine grace transforms behavior — the Christian life avoids both legalism and license.
body:
Paul's letter to the Galatians is a sustained argument against legalism. Having begun by the Spirit, he asks, are you now trying to finish by the flesh [verse:gal.3.3]? The Galatian churches were being pressured to add law-keeping to their faith, and Paul treats this not as a minor adjustment but as a different gospel entirely.

The foundational declaration is Ephesians 2: by grace you have been saved through faith, and this is not from yourselves — it is the gift of God, not by works, so that no one can boast [verse:eph.2.8-9]. Salvation is not earned. It cannot be earned. Any system that suggests otherwise has departed from the gospel.

But Paul immediately anticipates the opposite error. Shall we go on sinning so that grace may increase? By no means [verse:rom.6.1-2]. Grace is not license. The same letter that declares justification by faith apart from works insists that faith without works is dead [verse:jas.2.17]. James and Paul are not contradicting each other — they are addressing different errors.

Jesus himself navigated this tension. He fulfilled the law rather than abolishing it [verse:matt.5.17], yet he scandalized the rule-keepers by healing on the Sabbath, eating with sinners, and declaring that the Sabbath was made for man, not man for the Sabbath [verse:mark.2.27].

The mature Christian life holds both truths: I am saved by grace alone, and that grace compels me to live differently. Obedience flows from gratitude, not from fear of losing what grace has already secured.

verses.annotation: The definitive statement: salvation by grace through faith, not by works.
Paul's rebuke of legalism — beginning by Spirit and finishing by flesh is incoherent.
Grace is not a license to sin — Paul rejects antinomianism as forcefully as legalism.
Faith without corresponding action is dead — genuine belief produces visible change.
Jesus fulfills the law rather than abolishing it — the law points to him.
The Sabbath made for humanity, not humanity for the Sabbath — rules serve people, not the reverse.
Grace teaches believers to live self-controlled, upright lives — transformation is grace's purpose.

scholars.quote: Schreiner argues that Paul's theology holds justification and sanctification in an unbreakable union — grace that forgives also transforms, making both legalism and antinomianism distortions of the gospel.

Lincoln reads Ephesians 2:8-10 as a carefully constructed argument: verses 8-9 demolish works-righteousness, while verse 10 immediately establishes that believers are created for good works — the sequence is essential.
Davids emphasizes that James 2 is not opposing Paul but complementing him — James defines the kind of faith that saves as faith that acts, while Paul defines the basis of salvation as faith alone.
